narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 29.30, Year 1 school decile of 3rd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 2nd out of 10, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 75.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 academic decile ranking, which was in the bottom third of your school (far below the typical 5th or 6th decile), powerfully pushing the prediction toward expecting bar failure by signaling weaker late-stage performance. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile in the 3rd position (again below the average 5th or 6th), which further reinforced concerns about consistently subpar grades relative to peers. Your LSAT score provided the main counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success since it showed some potential despite being lower than the average of 36.70. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your grades placed you in lower deciles across both early and later years, which stood out as red flags even though your full-time status aligns with most admits and your middle-income background is common. These academic rankings carried the heaviest weight because they directly correlate with bar exam readiness in the model's view of past students. While no single factor dooms an application, the combination here tipped the scales decisively against you. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting any improvements or unique strengths like resilience from your background. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on, so view this as targeted feedback rather than a final verdict. With focused effort, you can reshape your profile to align better with high bar-pass predictors.
